message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Early identification of SARS-CoV-2 infection is important to guide quarantine and reduce transmission. This study evaluates the diagnostic performance of lung ultrasound (LUS), an affordable, consumable-free point-of-care tool, for COVID-19 screening.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This prospective observational cohort included adults presenting with cough and/or dyspnea at a SARS-CoV-2 screening center of Lausanne University Hospital between March 31
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    and May 8
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    , 2020. Investigators recorded standardized LUS images and videos in 10 lung zones per subject. Two blinded independent experts reviewed LUS recording and classified abnormal findings according to pre-specified criteria to investigate their predictive value to diagnose SARS-CoV-2 infection according to PCR on nasopharyngeal swabs (COVID
                    <jats:sup>pos</jats:sup>
                    vs COVID
                    <jats:sup>neg</jats:sup>
                    ). We finally combined LUS and clinical findings to derive a multivariate logistic regression diagnostic score.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Of 134 included patients, 23% (n=30/134) were COVID
                    <jats:sup>pos</jats:sup>
                    and 77% (n=103/134) were COVID
                    <jats:sup>neg</jats:sup>
                    ; 85%, (n=114/134) cases were previously healthy healthcare workers presenting within 2 to 5 days of symptom onset (IQR). Abnormal LUS findings were significantly more frequent in COVID
                    <jats:sup>pos</jats:sup>
                    compared to COVID
                    <jats:sup>neg</jats:sup>
                    (45% versus 26%, p=0.045) and mostly consisted of focal pathologic B-lines. Combining LUS findings in a multivariate logistic regression score had an area under the receiver-operating curve of 63.9% to detect COVID-19, but improved to 84.5% with the addition of clinical features
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID
                    <jats:sup>pos</jats:sup>
                    patients are significantly more likely to have lung pathology by LUS. Our findings have potential diagnostic value for COVID-19 at the point of care. Combination of clinical and LUS features showed promising results, which need confirmation in a larger study population.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What is already known on the subject</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Lung ultrasonography (LUS) is a consumable-free, easy-to-use, portable, non-radiating and non-invasive screening tool that can be performed at the bedside: its diagnostic performance for pneumonia has been established.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Recent studies conducted in emergency department showed a correlation between LUS findings and COVID-19 diagnosis.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What the study ads</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>This is the first study assessing the diagnostic performance of LUS for COVID-19 in outpatients with mild acute respiratory tract infection.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Mild COVID-19 patients are more likely to have lung pathology by LUS compared with COVID-19 negative.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Combination of clinical and LUS features showed promising results with a potential diagnostic value for COVID-19 at the point of care.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Point-of-care lung ultrasonography for early identification of mild COVID-19: a prospective cohort of outpatients in a Swiss screening center
